clusters.title: Trump escalates pressure on Cuba as US charges Raul Castro with murder
clusters.neutral_headline: US charges Cuba's Raúl Castro with murder over 1996 downing of two planes
bullet_summary: The ​United States announced murder charges against former Cuban President Raúl Castro on Wednesday, a major escalation in Washington's pressure campaign against the island's communist government
The ​United States announced murder charges against former Cuban President Raul Castro on Wednesday, a major escalation in Washington's pressure campaign against the island's communist government

items.title: Trump escalates pressure on Cuba as US charges Raul Castro with murder
items.standfirst: The ​United States announced murder charges against former Cuban President Raúl Castro on Wednesday, a major escalation in Washington's pressure campaign against the island's communist government. The indictment marks a new low in relations between the longtime Cold War rivalsand comes as U.S. President Donald Trump is pushingfor regime change in Cuba, where Castro's communists have been in charge since his late brother Fidel Castro led a revolution in 1959.
items.content: The ​United States announced murder charges against former Cuban President Raúl Castro on Wednesday, a major escalation in Washington's pressure campaign against the island's communist government. The indictment marks a new low in relations between the longtime Cold War rivalsand comes as U.S. President Donald Trump is pushingfor regime change in Cuba, where Castro's communists have been in charge since his late brother Fidel Castro led a revolution in 1959.

items.title: Why has US charged Cuba's Raul Castro with murder?
items.standfirst: The ​United States announced murder charges against former Cuban President Raul Castro on Wednesday, a major escalation in Washington's pressure campaign against the island's communist government. The charges stem from a 1996 ‌incident in which Cuban jets shot down planes operated by a group of Cuban exiles. France 24's Philip Turle takes a look at the allegations against Castro and why the charges have come now, three decades after the event.
items.content: The ​United States announced murder charges against former Cuban President Raul Castro on Wednesday, a major escalation in Washington's pressure campaign against the island's communist government. The charges stem from a 1996 ‌incident in which Cuban jets shot down planes operated by a group of Cuban exiles. France 24's Philip Turle takes a look at the allegations against Castro and why the charges have come now, three decades after the event.

items.title: U.S. indicts Cuba's Raúl Castro for murder over downing of planes in 1996
items.standfirst: Former Cuban leader Raúl Castro was indicted by a U.S. grand jury in connection with the Cuban military's fatal downing of two planes in 1996 — an escalation in the U.S. pressure campaign against the Cuban government.
items.content: Former Cuban leader Raúl Castro was indicted by a U.S. grand jury in connection with the Cuban military's fatal downing of two planes in 1996 — an escalation in the U.S. pressure campaign against the Cuban government.

items.title: US charges Cuba's Raúl Castro with murder over 1996 downing of two planes
items.standfirst: Cuba's current president says the charges against Castro - including conspiracy to kill US nationals and destruction of aircraft - are a "political manoeuvre".
items.content: Cuba's current president says the charges against Castro - including conspiracy to kill US nationals and destruction of aircraft - are a "political manoeuvre".

items.title: Trump says ‘there won’t be escalation’ with Cuba after US indictment of former president Raúl Castro – as it happened
items.standfirst: Cuba’s government has also accused US of hypocrisy after acting US attorney general announced indictment of former Cuban president over downed planesUS indicts former Cuban president Raúl Castro as it seeks to oust regimeTrump news at a glance: US indicts Raúl Castro, ratcheting up Cuba tensionsSign up for the Breaking News US emailIn response to a question on Wednesday morning about how long Benjamin Netanyahu, the prime minister of Israel, will hold on before re-starting strikes on Iran, Trump said: “He’s fine. He’ll do whatever I want him to do.”Trump also cited a poll that gave him 99% approval in Israel. Guardian US has not yet verified this poll. Continue reading...
items.content: Cuba’s government has also accused US of hypocrisy after acting US attorney general announced indictment of former Cuban president over downed planesUS indicts former Cuban president Raúl Castro as it seeks to oust regimeTrump news at a glance: US indicts Raúl Castro, ratcheting up Cuba tensionsSign up for the Breaking News US emailIn response to a question on Wednesday morning about how long Benjamin Netanyahu, the prime minister of Israel, will hold on before re-starting strikes on Iran, Trump said: “He’s fine. He’ll do whatever I want him to do.”Trump also cited a poll that gave him 99% approval in Israel. Guardian US has not yet verified this poll. Continue reading...

items.title: Justice Department charges Raul Castro with murder
items.standfirst: A Florida grand jury indicted Raul Castro, the former leader of Cuba’s Communist regime, with murder and other charges for the 1996 shootdown of American humanitarian airplanes. NBC News Gabe Gutierrez reports.
items.content: A Florida grand jury indicted Raul Castro, the former leader of Cuba’s Communist regime, with murder and other charges for the 1996 shootdown of American humanitarian airplanes. NBC News Gabe Gutierrez reports.

items.title: US indicts former Cuban president Raúl Castro as it seeks to oust regime
items.standfirst: Charges filed in Miami against 94-year-old for allegedly shooting down exiles’ planes in 1996The United States issued a federal criminal indictment against Raúl Castro, Cuba’s former president, and five others on Wednesday in a significant escalation of the Trump administration’s campaign to oust the country’s six-decades-old communist regime.The 94-year-old political figurehead was charged in Miami, Florida, with conspiracy to kill US nationals, four counts of murder and two counts of destruction of aircraft. Continue reading...
items.content: Charges filed in Miami against 94-year-old for allegedly shooting down exiles’ planes in 1996The United States issued a federal criminal indictment against Raúl Castro, Cuba’s former president, and five others on Wednesday in a significant escalation of the Trump administration’s campaign to oust the country’s six-decades-old communist regime.The 94-year-old political figurehead was charged in Miami, Florida, with conspiracy to kill US nationals, four counts of murder and two counts of destruction of aircraft. Continue reading...

items.title: Raúl Castro indictment brings back history of Cuba’s downing of U.S. civilian planes
items.standfirst: Cuba shot down two Brothers to the Rescue U.S. civilian planes in 1996, killing four Cuban Americans and drawing swift condemnation from the U.S. and the international community.
items.content: Cuba shot down two Brothers to the Rescue U.S. civilian planes in 1996, killing four Cuban Americans and drawing swift condemnation from the U.S. and the international community.

items.title: Blanche faces questions over how Castro will stand trial in the U.S.
items.standfirst: Acting Attorney General Todd Blanche said that an arrest warrant has been issued for former Cuban leader Raúl Castro over a 1996 incident in which Cuban jets shot down two civilian planes, killing four Cuban Americans. NBC News’ Gabe Gutierrez reports on how Blanche responded to questions over how Castro is expected to stand trial in the U.S.
items.content: Acting Attorney General Todd Blanche said that an arrest warrant has been issued for former Cuban leader Raúl Castro over a 1996 incident in which Cuban jets shot down two civilian planes, killing four Cuban Americans. NBC News’ Gabe Gutierrez reports on how Blanche responded to questions over how Castro is expected to stand trial in the U.S.

items.title: The story of Cuba's 1996 shootdown that led to Raúl Castro's indictment
items.standfirst: Thirty years ago, a Cuban fighter jet shot down two civilian planes operated by Florida-based exile group Brothers to the Rescue, an incident that inflamed U.S.-Cuba relations.
items.content: Thirty years ago, a Cuban fighter jet shot down two civilian planes operated by Florida-based exile group Brothers to the Rescue, an incident that inflamed U.S.-Cuba relations.

items.title: Former Cuba president Raul Castro charged with murder in US
items.standfirst: Raul Castro, Cuba's former president, has been charged with murder in the US in connection with a military airstrike against two civilian planes, which killed four people.
items.content: Raul Castro, Cuba's former president, has been charged with murder in the US in connection with a military airstrike against two civilian planes, which killed four people.

clusters.title: Far-right Israeli minister condemned for taunting handcuffed Gaza flotilla activists
clusters.neutral_headline: Israeli minister Ben-Gvir's video of Gaza flotilla activists sparks outcry
bullet_summary: Three activists were taken to hospital as result of Israeli violence, lawyers representing the group said
Reported by BBC World, France24, Guardian World and others

items.title: Far-right Israeli minister condemned for taunting handcuffed Gaza flotilla activists
items.standfirst: France and Italy are among the countries that have criticised a video showing Itamar Ben-Gvir taunting dozens of activists detained at an Israeli port.
items.content: France and Italy are among the countries that have criticised a video showing Itamar Ben-Gvir taunting dozens of activists detained at an Israeli port.

items.title: Israeli minister Ben-Gvir's video of Gaza flotilla activists sparks outcry
items.standfirst: Detained activists, their hands tied and forced to kneel as the Israeli national anthem is belted out in the background. Israeli National Security Minister Itamar Ben-Gvir released footage on X of him taunting the activists from a Gaza-bound flotilla. The video sparked widespread international condemnations. FRANCE 24’s Gabrielle Nadler reports.
items.content: Detained activists, their hands tied and forced to kneel as the Israeli national anthem is belted out in the background. Israeli National Security Minister Itamar Ben-Gvir released footage on X of him taunting the activists from a Gaza-bound flotilla. The video sparked widespread international condemnations. FRANCE 24’s Gabrielle Nadler reports.

items.title: Middle East live: Israel begins deporting Gaza flotilla activists after video outcry
items.standfirst: Israel on Thursday began deporting hundreds of activists who attempted to breach the naval blockade of Gaza, according to a legal organisation working with the humanitarian flotilla. It followed an international outcry over a video clip of Israeli National Security Minister Itmar Ben-Gvir heckling the detained activists. Follow our liveblog for the latest developments.
items.content: Israel on Thursday began deporting hundreds of activists who attempted to breach the naval blockade of Gaza, according to a legal organisation working with the humanitarian flotilla. It followed an international outcry over a video clip of Israeli National Security Minister Itmar Ben-Gvir heckling the detained activists. Follow our liveblog for the latest developments.

items.title: Israeli security minister stirs diplomatic outrage with flotilla activist abuse video
items.standfirst: Far-right figure Itamar Ben-Gvir shares footage of himself taunting bound international detaineesIsrael’s far-right national security minister, Itamar Ben-Gvir, has sparked a diplomatic crisis by publishing footage of Israeli security forces abusing international activists who were detained as they tried to sail to Gaza with aid.Three activists were taken to hospital as result of Israeli violence, lawyers representing the group said. They were subsequently discharged. Dozens of others have suspected broken ribs, resulting in breathing problems. Continue reading...
items.content: Far-right figure Itamar Ben-Gvir shares footage of himself taunting bound international detaineesIsrael’s far-right national security minister, Itamar Ben-Gvir, has sparked a diplomatic crisis by publishing footage of Israeli security forces abusing international activists who were detained as they tried to sail to Gaza with aid.Three activists were taken to hospital as result of Israeli violence, lawyers representing the group said. They were subsequently discharged. Dozens of others have suspected broken ribs, resulting in breathing problems. Continue reading...

items.title: Israeli security minister taunts detained flotilla activists as police filmed forcing them to kneel
items.standfirst: Israeli police have been filmed forcing activists to kneel on the ground with their hands tied behind their backs while the country's national security minister tells them they should be imprisoned for a long time.
items.content: Israeli police have been filmed forcing activists to kneel on the ground with their hands tied behind their backs while the country's national security minister tells them they should be imprisoned for a long time.

items.standfirst: In its latest update, the World Health Organization says there have now been 139 suspected deaths and 600 cases.
items.content: In its latest update, the World Health Organization says there have now been 139 suspected deaths and 600 cases.

items.title: Ebola death toll rises as two Americans are flown to Europe for monitoring
items.standfirst: The suspected death toll from the Ebola outbreak in central Africa rose again Wednesday as a group of potentially exposed Americans was arriving in Europe for monitoring and the World Health Organization warned a vaccine was still months away.
items.content: The suspected death toll from the Ebola outbreak in central Africa rose again Wednesday as a group of potentially exposed Americans was arriving in Europe for monitoring and the World Health Organization warned a vaccine was still months away.

bullet_summary: Reported by France24, Bloomberg Technology

items.title: Samsung union suspends strike after reaching tentative pay deal
items.standfirst: Samsung Electronics' union said it would suspend a strike shortly before it was set to begin on Thursday after it reached a tentative pay deal with the company, averting action that threatened South Korea's economy and global chip supply.
items.content: Samsung Electronics' union said it would suspend a strike shortly before it was set to begin on Thursday after it reached a tentative pay deal with the company, averting action that threatened South Korea's economy and global chip supply.

items.title: Samsung Chip Workers to Get Average $340,000 Bonus in AI Boom
items.standfirst: Samsung Electronics Co. will distribute about 40 trillion won ($26.6 billion) to chip employees as a bonus for this year after the company struck a last-minute deal with labor unions to avert a strike.
items.content: Samsung Electronics Co. will distribute about 40 trillion won ($26.6 billion) to chip employees as a bonus for this year after the company struck a last-minute deal with labor unions to avert a strike.

items.title: Samsung Reaches 11th-Hour Deal With Union to Avoid Strike
items.standfirst: In a last-minute reversal, Samsung Electronics Co. reached a tentative deal with its labor union, averting a potentially crippling strike that had been scheduled to start Thursday at the world’s largest memory chipmaker.
items.content: In a last-minute reversal, Samsung Electronics Co. reached a tentative deal with its labor union, averting a potentially crippling strike that had been scheduled to start Thursday at the world’s largest memory chipmaker.

clusters.title: Embattled Bolivian leader announces cabinet reshuffle in bid to end crisis
clusters.neutral_headline: Bolivia’s president reshuffles cabinet amid anti-government protests
bullet_summary: Bolivian President Rodrigo Paz on Wednesday announced plans to reshuffle his cabinet to make it more inclusive following weeks of anti-government protests
Bolivian President Rodrigo Paz has announced a cabinet reshuffle and other measures as protests escalate

items.title: Embattled Bolivian leader announces cabinet reshuffle in bid to end crisis
items.standfirst: Bolivian President Rodrigo Paz on Wednesday announced plans to reshuffle his cabinet to make it more inclusive following weeks of anti-government protests. Bolivians began demonstrating and erecting roadblocks three weeks ago to demand salary increases, stable fuel supplies and measures to mitigate the worst economic crisis in 40 years.
items.content: Bolivian President Rodrigo Paz on Wednesday announced plans to reshuffle his cabinet to make it more inclusive following weeks of anti-government protests. Bolivians began demonstrating and erecting roadblocks three weeks ago to demand salary increases, stable fuel supplies and measures to mitigate the worst economic crisis in 40 years.

clusters.title: SpaceX unveils plans to go public, with $1.75 trillion valuation
clusters.neutral_headline: SpaceX unveils plans to go public, with $1.75 trillion valuation
bullet_summary: Reported by France24, Guardian Business

items.title: SpaceX unveils plans to go public, with $1.75 trillion valuation
items.standfirst: Elon Musk's rocket and satellite company has publicly filed details about its upcoming initial public offering, highlighting both the company's ambitious goals in space and its financial results. Among the group's long-term goals are the construction of data centres in space and creating a large human settlement on Mars. Also in this edition: Samsung and its workers find a last-minute deal to suspend a planned 18-day strike.
items.content: Elon Musk's rocket and satellite company has publicly filed details about its upcoming initial public offering, highlighting both the company's ambitious goals in space and its financial results. Among the group's long-term goals are the construction of data centres in space and creating a large human settlement on Mars. Also in this edition: Samsung and its workers find a last-minute deal to suspend a planned 18-day strike.

items.title: SpaceX reveals plan for $1.75tn stock market debut that could make Musk a trillionaire
items.standfirst: Elon Musk’s rocket and satellite operations company, with extensive contracts with US, to go public next monthSpaceX unveiled its plans to list publicly on the US stock market Wednesday, disclosing its investor prospectus and revealing details about its financials for the first time. Elon Musk’s rocket and satellite operations company will go public on the Nasdaq exchange at a valuation of about $1.75tn under the symbol SPCX, likely on 12 June. It is seeking up to $80bn in investment.The company, which is the world’s most prominent rocket maker and which has extensive contracts with the US government, confidentially filed for an IPO last month. The filing allowed for a period of regulatory review before the details became public. Continue reading...
items.content: Elon Musk’s rocket and satellite operations company, with extensive contracts with US, to go public next monthSpaceX unveiled its plans to list publicly on the US stock market Wednesday, disclosing its investor prospectus and revealing details about its financials for the first time. Elon Musk’s rocket and satellite operations company will go public on the Nasdaq exchange at a valuation of about $1.75tn under the symbol SPCX, likely on 12 June. It is seeking up to $80bn in investment.The company, which is the world’s most prominent rocket maker and which has extensive contracts with the US government, confidentially filed for an IPO last month. The filing allowed for a period of regulatory review before the details became public. Continue reading...

clusters.title: Trump risks provoking China's ire with planned call with Taiwan leader
clusters.neutral_headline: Taiwan’s President Lai says he would be happy to speak with Donald Trump
bullet_summary: US President ​Donald Trump said on Wednesday he would speak with Taiwan's President Lai Ching-te, an unprecedented move for a US leader that could roil US relations with China
Reported by France24, Al Jazeera

items.title: Trump risks provoking China's ire with planned call with Taiwan leader
items.standfirst: US President ​Donald Trump said on Wednesday he would speak with Taiwan's President Lai Ching-te, an unprecedented move for a US leader that could roil US relations with China. US and Taiwanese presidents have not spoken directly since Washington shifted diplomatic recognition to Beijing from ​Taipei in 1979. France 24's correspondent in Taiwan Jan Camenzind Broomby takes a look at how likely it is the call will go ahead and what the two leaders might discuss.
items.content: US President ​Donald Trump said on Wednesday he would speak with Taiwan's President Lai Ching-te, an unprecedented move for a US leader that could roil US relations with China. US and Taiwanese presidents have not spoken directly since Washington shifted diplomatic recognition to Beijing from ​Taipei in 1979. France 24's correspondent in Taiwan Jan Camenzind Broomby takes a look at how likely it is the call will go ahead and what the two leaders might discuss.

items.title: Taiwan’s President Lai says he would be happy to speak with Donald Trump
items.standfirst: US and Taiwan leaders have not spoken since Washington shifted diplomatic recognition from Taipei to Beijing in 1979.
items.content: US and Taiwan leaders have not spoken since Washington shifted diplomatic recognition from Taipei to Beijing in 1979.

clusters.title: Beyond Plastics says tracked Starbucks cups didn’t reach a recycling facility
clusters.neutral_headline: Beyond Plastics says tracked Starbucks cups didn’t reach a recycling facility
bullet_summary: A three-month investigation found that none of the tracked Starbucks cold-beverage cups placed in in-store recycling bins ended up at a recycling facility
Reported by Reuters Top News, Guardian Business

items.title: Beyond Plastics says tracked Starbucks cups didn’t reach a recycling facility
items.standfirst: A three-month investigation found that none of the tracked Starbucks cold-beverage cups placed in in-store recycling bins ended up at a recycling facility. Instead, the cups were tracked to landfills, incinerators, waste-transfer stations, and material recovery facilities, raising questions about Starbucks’ “widely recyclable” claim. [beyondplastics.org]
items.content: A three-month investigation found that none of the tracked Starbucks cold-beverage cups placed in in-store recycling bins ended up at a recycling facility. Instead, the cups were tracked to landfills, incinerators, waste-transfer stations, and material recovery facilities, raising questions about Starbucks’ “widely recyclable” claim. [beyondplastics.org]

items.title: Starbucks does not recycle plastic cups it claims are ‘widely recyclable’, report says
items.standfirst: Watchdog group glued trackers to 53 of the chain’s cups across nine states and found none ended up at a recycling facilityIf you attach a GPS tracker to a “widely recyclable” plastic Starbucks cup and drop it in an in-store recycling bin, you might expect it to end up in a recycling plant, but the environmental watchdog organization Beyond Plastics says that’s not the case in a new report.Starbucks announced that their plastic cups were now considered “widely recyclable” earlier this year, according to How2Recycle, a group affiliated with the consumer packaging industry that helps private companies label their packaging with recycling options. The coffee giant touted the achievement as a “big milestone, with huge impact”. Continue reading...
items.content: Watchdog group glued trackers to 53 of the chain’s cups across nine states and found none ended up at a recycling facilityIf you attach a GPS tracker to a “widely recyclable” plastic Starbucks cup and drop it in an in-store recycling bin, you might expect it to end up in a recycling plant, but the environmental watchdog organization Beyond Plastics says that’s not the case in a new report.Starbucks announced that their plastic cups were now considered “widely recyclable” earlier this year, according to How2Recycle, a group affiliated with the consumer packaging industry that helps private companies label their packaging with recycling options. The coffee giant touted the achievement as a “big milestone, with huge impact”. Continue reading...

items.standfirst: Xi Jinping welcomed Vladimir Putin to Beijing with pomp and pageantry, just days after hosting Donald Trump. But as Russia’s war in Ukraine makes Moscow increasingly dependent on China, and western leaders thaw relations with Beijing, what does the power imbalance mean for Xi and Putin’s relationship? items.title: China and Russia sign agreements strengthening their ties after Xi-Putin meeting
items.standfirst: Chinese leader Xi Jinping welcomed Russian President Vladimir Putin to Beijing for a meeting one week after Xi met with President Donald Trump for a summit. NBC News' Janis Mackey Frayer reports on how China is seeking to project itself as a stabilizing force on the world stage.
items.content: Chinese leader Xi Jinping welcomed Russian President Vladimir Putin to Beijing for a meeting one week after Xi met with President Donald Trump for a summit. NBC News' Janis Mackey Frayer reports on how China is seeking to project itself as a stabilizing force on the world stage.

items.title: Putin and Xi say ties are at ‘unprecedented’ high at friendly summit after Trump visit
items.standfirst: China-Russia relations are at a “historic high,” Chinese leader Xi Jinping said as he met with Russian President Vladimir Putin in Beijing days after hosting U.S. President Donald Trump.
items.content: China-Russia relations are at a “historic high,” Chinese leader Xi Jinping said as he met with Russian President Vladimir Putin in Beijing days after hosting U.S. President Donald Trump.

items.title: Goodbye Trump, hello Putin: China’s Xi welcomes Russian leader facing pressure at home
items.standfirst: Vladimir Putin landed in China on Tuesday to a warm welcome from his “dear friend” President Xi Jinping, but he arrived with the heavy burden of a war he increasingly seems unable to win.
items.content: Vladimir Putin landed in China on Tuesday to a warm welcome from his “dear friend” President Xi Jinping, but he arrived with the heavy burden of a war he increasingly seems unable to win.
